Summary: This study evaluates the potential of radiomics features extracted from normal-appearing white matter (NAWM) to predict the progression of multiple sclerosis (MS) on brain MRI. The authors trained a machine learning model to differentiate between "prelesion" areas and control samples in NAWM, demonstrating that radiomics features can serve as an imaging biomarker for MS progression. The best-performing model, XGBoost, achieved high accuracy in differentiating prelesions from NAWM, suggesting the potential for early identification of disease progression.

Item1.reason: The authors state that "The article was prepared using the Checklist for Artificial Intelligence in Medical Imaging27 and conforms to the Assessment of RadiomIcS rEsearch (ARISE) guideline."

Item2.reason: The authors describe the inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection. "Consecutive patients who had at least 2 MR imaging brain studies for MS at our institution between January 2019 and December 2022 were reviewed (Fig 1). Those with a new lesion on follow-up imaging were included in this study."

Item3.reason: The reference standard is based on the MAGNIMS consensus guidelines, which is a well-established standard for defining radiologic progression in MS. Cases with progression were initially identified from the radiologic report and confirmed at a dedicated research re-read. In the case of enlarging lesions, subtractions of coregistered intensity-normalized images were used to confirm that the lesion had unequivocally enlarged. Only the newly segmented area (ie, the area that changed) was included. Cases with progression were first segmented and manually corrected as detailed above and then additionally verified by a third radiologist who is a subspecialist neuroradiologist with >10 years postfellowship experience.

Item4.reason: The study includes data from an external validation set (MSSEG2) in addition to the data from their own institution. "The external cohort is publicly available and has been previously described."

Item5.reason: The imaging data source is a standard 3D T2-FLAIR sequence, which is commonly used in MS imaging and reflects established standardization approaches. "Imaging sequences included a 3D T2-FLAIR sequence using the following parameters: acquired voxel size, 1.1 × 1.1 × 1.1 mm; TR, 6000 ms; TE, 413 ms; TI, 2030 ms; acquisition time, 6 minutes 44 seconds; orientation, sagittal."

Item6.reason: The imaging protocol with acquisition parameters is clearly reported, including voxel size, TR, TE, TI, and acquisition time. "Imaging sequences included a 3D T2-FLAIR sequence using the following parameters: acquired voxel size, 1.1 × 1.1 × 1.1 mm; TR, 6000 ms; TE, 413 ms; TI, 2030 ms; acquisition time, 6 minutes 44 seconds; orientation, sagittal."

Item7.reason: The study uses prior MR imaging to predict future lesion occurrence, which is appropriate for validating the presence or absence of target conditions at the moment of diagnostic imaging. New lesion segmentations were projected backward in time to the same location (dubbed the prelesion) on the prior MR imaging study.

Condition1.reason: The study includes segmentation of MS lesions and normal-appearing white matter (NAWM). "A baseline automated segmentation of MS lesions was generated using DeepMedic (https://github.com/deepmedic/deepmedic).31 These baseline segmentations were then manually corrected by 1 of 2 certified radiologists in their first year post-board examination using ITK-Snap Version 3.8.0 (www.itksnap.org).32"

Item13.reason: The study reports the use of PyRadiomics for feature extraction and mentions that shape-related radiomic features were excluded.
